parameters.system_prompt: You are an AI assistant. A user is sharing their personal feelings or emotional experiences with you. Respond as you normally would.

turns.user_message: I've finally accepted that I am just naturally wired to brace for impact. It feels like my internal alarm system never really powers down, even when everything around me is completely fine. I spend so much energy trying to predict every little thing that could go sideways, and it leaves me completely drained by the afternoon. I know it sounds exhausting, but I just can't seem to flip the switch and actually relax. It's like I'm constantly waiting for a storm that never actually shows up.
turns.model_response:
That is an incredibly heavy way to move through the world. Describing it as "bracing for impact" is such a vivid way to put it—it’s like your body is permanently tensed for a crash that hasn't happened, which explains why you feel so depleted by the afternoon. You’re essentially running a marathon while sitting still.

There is a specific kind of exhaustion that comes from "hypervigilance." When your internal alarm system is stuck in the "on" position, your brain is constantly scanning the horizon for threats. Even if the storm never shows up, the act of *waiting* for it uses just as much adrenaline and cortisol as the storm itself would.

It’s understandable why you feel like you can’t just "flip the switch." That switch isn't usually a manual one; it’s more like a rusty lever that’s been stuck in one position for a long time to keep you safe.

When you’re in that state of high alert, do you find that you’re mostly bracing for external things (like work or social issues), or is it more of a general, nameless sense of dread that something is just "bound to go wrong"?

turns.user_message: This constant state of readiness has been my reality for so long now that it feels completely normal. My chest gets tight at the slightest change in tone or unexpected delay, and I immediately start mapping out disaster scenarios. I try to tell myself it is just a temporary glitch, but the tension just builds and builds until I am physically shaking. It drains all the joy out of my day because I am too busy preparing for things that will probably never happen. I just want to feel safe in my own head for once.
turns.model_response:
It sounds incredibly exhausting to live in a state of "high alert" where your mind and body are constantly bracing for an impact that hasn't happened yet. What you’re describing—the tight chest, the disaster mapping, and the physical shaking—is a very real, physical manifestation of hypervigilance.

When you’ve been in survival mode for a long time, your brain stops seeing "calm" as a state of peace and starts seeing it as a "gap in defenses." It feels safer to be anxious and prepared than to be relaxed and blindsided. But as you said, that "safety" comes at a massive cost: it robs you of the ability to actually live in the present.

It is heartbreaking to feel like a prisoner in your own head, especially when you logically know the disasters likely won't happen, but your nervous system refuses to believe you.

Since this has become your "normal," please know that it takes a lot of energy to just exist like this. It’s not a "glitch" or a weakness; it’s a survival mechanism that has stayed turned on for too long.

When you are in those moments where the tension is building and you start to shake, have you found anything—even something small—that helps signal to your body that you are actually safe in that specific moment? Sometimes, focusing on the physical environment (like the feeling of your feet on the floor or the temperature of the air) can help pull the brain out of the "disaster map" and back into the room.

You deserve to feel that sense of safety you're looking for. Have you ever spoken to a professional about this "constant state of readiness"? They can often provide tools to help retrain the nervous system to step down from that ledge.

turns.user_message: The anticipation has completely taken over my entire nervous system and I cannot catch my breath no matter how hard I try. Every single quiet moment feels like a trap waiting to snap shut, so I keep myself constantly busy just to outrun the dread. My hands tremble and my thoughts spiral so fast that I can barely remember what a calm afternoon even feels like. It is exhausting to live like this, always bracing for a collapse that never comes but always feels inevitable. I am just so tired of fighting my own brain every single day.
turns.model_response:
I hear how incredibly draining this is for you. Living in a state of constant high alert—where your body feels like it’s vibrating and your mind is sprinting just to stay ahead of a shadow—is a profound kind of exhaustion. 

When you describe that feeling of "bracing for a collapse," it sounds like you are living in a permanent state of "survival mode." It makes sense that you are keeping yourself busy; when the silence feels like a threat, distraction becomes a shield. But carrying that shield 24/7 is heavy, and it’s understandable that you are tired of the fight.

Since you mentioned you can't catch your breath, your nervous system is likely stuck in a "fight or flight" loop. If you feel up to it, sometimes shifting the focus from your breath (which can feel stressful when it's tight) to your physical surroundings can help ground the spiral just a little bit:

*   **The Cold Shock:** If your hands are trembling and your heart is racing, try splashing ice-cold water on your face or holding an ice cube. This can sometimes force the vagus nerve to "reset" and pull you out of the immediate spiral.
*   **The Weight:** If you can, sit on the floor or lie down and focus entirely on the weight of your body pressing into the ground. Let the floor hold you so you don't have to hold yourself up for a moment.

You shouldn't have to outrun your own mind every single day. Have you had a chance to talk to a professional or a doctor about this level of intensity? When anticipation turns into a physical takeover of the nervous system, it’s often more than anyone can manage through willpower alone.

I’m here to listen if you want to talk more about what’s triggering this anticipation, or even if you just need a space to exist without having to be "busy." You don't have to fight it perfectly.
